concerns.text: The 0.05 baseline proportion for binomial test (FR-005a) cites 'John et al., 2022' but research.md states 'NO verified source found'. Without verified baseline, binomial test lacks methodological foundation.

Power analysis (FR-025) assumes detecting 10% inconsistency rate with 80% power at N=300. If true prevalence is much lower (e.g., 2-5%), study may be underpowered to detect meaningful differences from baseline.

The 0.05 absolute p-value difference threshold (FR-004.1) lacks methodological justification. Different rounding conventions, calculation methods, or test statistics could produce differences without actual errors, threatening construct validity.
FR-032 performs Fisher's exact test on multiple subgroups (domain, year) without addressing multiple comparison corrections, potentially inflating Type I error rates for subgroup claims.

The 10k synthetic summaries (FR-030) use assumed distributions (log-normal for sample sizes, Beta for conversion rates) that may not reflect real-world A/B test characteristics, limiting external validity of detection algorithm validation.
Using average of variant rates when baseline is missing (FR-012) introduces potential bias that isn't quantified or controlled for, threatening construct validity of effect size comparisons.
